methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $65M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Peak Energy, founded by Landon Mossburg and Liam O'Connor, is a Series A-stage climate and energy company seeking valuation following $65M in total fundraising. The relevant peer universe comprises 9 private companies and 1 public comparable (LNZA), with three same-stage, same-sector peers directly informing analysis: Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M).

Two triangulation methods were applied. The stage-progression method uses the $65M raised as a valuation proxy and applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A advancement, implying a $130M valuation. The peer-median method references the three direct comparables, which exhibit a $110M–$200M range with a $150M median. This method directly implies $150M.

Synthesizing both approaches yields a triangulated range of $110M (low) to $172M (high), with a $140M median. The two methods converge closely, with stage-progression at $130M and peer-median at $150M, suggesting modest confidence in the $140M midpoint. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the three-peer sample, variation in burn rates and runway across the peer set, and the applicability of public market comparables given the sector's high capital intensity and long development cycles. The wide peer range ($110M–$200M) reflects material differences in traction or investor expectations among stage-equivalent players.
